summary: DHH admits to being a very late adopter of LinkedIn after nearly twenty years of avoiding it, having dismissed it as a lame business version of Facebook. He argues that LinkedIn's perceived lameness has become its greatest strength, as the professional norms constrain people's behavior in ways that make discourse more pleasant than Twitter's toxic free-for-all. He acknowledges the irony of celebrating compartmentalized personas after years of combative Twitter engagement. The post concludes that the 'business casual' nature of LinkedIn connections is preferable to the unfiltered chaos of platforms where people bring their whole selves.
key_points: LinkedIn's professional norms and social restraint have become virtues as other platforms descended into toxicity
The collapse of boundaries between personal and professional identity on Twitter made discourse worse for everyone
Even 'fake' professional personas are preferable to being subjected to people's full unfiltered personalities
DHH acknowledges the irony of celebrating compartmentalization after years of combative Twitter participation
A throttled, curated connection is more valuable than full-bandwidth authenticity online
money_quotes: The restraint that people show in their virtual khakis and button-downs on a business network is apparently exactly the antidote we need in 2022 to stay even remotely within the boundaries of respectable exchanges online.
It's a place where people are inclined to bring their work self to the conversation. A place almost entirely free of the endless, droning moral grandstanding that has taken over Twitter.
It just doesn't matter if you don't have to know!
The entirety of someone's personality and predilections were suddenly thrust upon you, whether you wanted to know or not. And mostly, I can assuredly say, you did not!
After a solid decade duking it out on Twitter in plenty campaigns that had more than a pale moral hue to them, I would celebrate the compartmentalization of someone's public persona.
If we absolutely must use the internet to 'connect', then I welcome a throttled connection.
Many of our brightest dreams have turned into our darkest nightmares.
Rather than the full-bandwidth bananas bonkers nonsense of Your Whole Self.
key_insight: Social platforms benefit from constraints on self-expression — the professional norms that make LinkedIn feel 'fake' are precisely what make it more functional than platforms that encourage radical authenticity.
